sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: UP.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 29, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: - LEFT moved player from [26,14]-[30,18] to [20,14]-[24,18]. LEFT acts as "back" or moves to previous room. - Player is now at [20,14]-[24,18]. Need to find the correct path to the goal. - From earlier, DOWN from [20,14]-[24,18] goes to [26,14]-[30,18], which is a dead end. - Need to find another path. Try RIGHT from [20,14]-[24,18].
- Plan: Try RIGHT 3 times to [8,32]-[12,36], then DOWN to enter the white corridor at cols 30-36.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 28 of about 78 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Interesting! The player moved from [20,14]-[24,18] to [20,20]-[24,24]. So UP moved the player RIGHT, from cols 14-18 to cols 20-24. The row stayed at 20-24.
Wait, but I pressed UP, not RIGHT. The player moved from [20,14]-[24,18] to [20,20]-[24,24], which is RIGHT 6 cols. But I pressed UP.
This is confusing. Let me re-examine the game mechanics.
Actually, looking at the ASCII output:
Row 20: WBBBBBBBbbbbbBBB
- Col 12: W
- Cols 13-19: BBBBBBB (7 chars)
- Cols 20-24: bbbbbb (6 chars, player)
- Cols 25-27: BBB (3 chars)
So the player is at cols 20-24 (or 20-25 based on 6 chars). The boundary says [20,20]-[24,24], which is cols 20-24 (5 cols).
The player moved from [20,14]-[24,18] to [20,20]-[24,24]. This is RIGHT 6 cols (from 14-18 to 20-24).
But I pressed UP. So UP moves the player RIGHT in this context?
Actually, I think the game's direction keys are relative to the maze layout, not the screen. The maze might be rotated or the direction keys might be mapped differently.
Let me re-examine the earlier moves:
- DOWN from [8,14]-[12,18] to [14,14]-[18,18]: DOWN moved the player DOWN 6 rows.
- DOWN from [14,14]-[18,18] to [20,14]-[24,18]: DOWN moved the player DOWN 6 rows.
- DOWN from [20,14]-[24,18] to [26,14]-[30,18]: DOWN moved the player DOWN 6 rows.
- LEFT from [26,14]-[30,18] to [20,14]-[24,18]: LEFT moved the player UP 6 rows (back to previous room).
- RIGHT from [20,14]-[24,18]: didn't move (blocked).
- UP from [20,14]-[24,18] to [20,20]-[24,24]: UP moved the player RIGHT 6 cols.
So the direction keys seem to be:
- DOWN: moves player DOWN (to next room below)
- LEFT: moves player UP (back to previous room above)
- UP: moves player RIGHT (to next room to the right)
- RIGHT: moves player LEFT (back to previous room to the left)? Or maybe RIGHT is blocked in this direction.
Wait, this doesn't make sense. Let me re-examine.
Actually, I think the direction keys are relative to the maze's path, not the screen. The maze is a linear path, and the direction keys move the player along the path.
- DOWN: moves player forward along the path (DOWN on screen)
- UP: moves player forward along the path (RIGHT on screen)
- LEFT: moves player backward along the path (UP on screen)
- RIGHT: moves player backward along the path (LEFT on screen)? Or maybe RIGHT is blocked.
Actually, looking at the moves:
- DOWN: [8,14]-[12,18] -> [14,14]-[18,18] -> [20,14]-[24,18] -> [26,14]-[30,18] (forward, DOWN on screen)
- LEFT: [26,14]-[30,18] -> [20,14]-[24,18] (backward, UP on screen)
- UP: [20,14]-[24,18] -> [20,20]-[24,24] (forward, RIGHT on screen)
So the path goes:
[8,14]-[12,18] (start) -> DOWN -> [14,14]-[18,18] -> DOWN -> [20,14]-[24,18] -> DOWN -> [26,14]-[30,18] (dead end)
OR
[20,14]-[24,18] -> UP -> [20,20]-[24,24] (new direction)
So from [20,14]-[24,18], the player can go DOWN (to [26,14]-[30,18], dead end) or UP (to [20,20]-[24,24], new direction).
The player is now at [20,20]-[24,24]. Let me continue exploring from here.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model:
- UP moved player from [20,14]-[24,18] to [20,20]-[24,24]. UP moves player RIGHT along the path.
- Path so far: start -> DOWN -> DOWN -> DOWN (dead end) -> LEFT (back) -> UP (new direction)
- Player now at [20,20]-[24,24]. Need to continue along the path.
- Direction mapping: DOWN=forward-down, UP=forward-right, LEFT=backward-up, RIGHT=backward-left?

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check player position and area around
seg = current_frame.segmentation
nodes = seg['nodes']
blue_player = [n for n in nodes if n['color'] == 'b' and n['pixels'] == 24]
print(f"Player position: {blue_player[0]['boundary']}")

# Check area around player
ascii_lines = current_frame.ascii.split('\n')
p = blue_player[0]['boundary']
r_min, r_max = p[0][0], p[2][0]
c_min, c_max = p[0][1], p[2][1]
print(f"\nPlayer area (rows {r_min-2}-{r_max+2}, cols {c_min-2}-{c_max+10}):")
for i in range(max(0, r_min-2), min(63, r_max+3)):
    print(f"{i:2d}: {ascii_lines[i][max(0,c_min-2):min(64,c_max+10)]}")

# Try DOWN to continue forward
action(['DOWN'])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
